Lots of debate on whether hiking the minimum wage, or even whether we should have one, is a good idea. Other articles say there's no evidence of long term job loss from the minimum wage. Is this the same as saying there's no evidence of a lack of job gain? Could the minimum wage lead to jobs not being created which otherwise would be? A long dead economist said we should consider "not just that which is seen but that which is not seen" before deciding whether a policy is a good one. If a job's pay is largely determined by its marginal productivity,will it exist if, as for the lower skilled or entry level, this is less than the minimum wage? Is it wise to require higher pay for low productivity jobs or would it be better to focus on raising worker productivity? The argument seems to be that raising the minimum wage will put more money into the hands of consumers whose spending will lift the economy and help us in the long term. If that is the major factor, why stop at $15/hr?

Having dealt with a large amount of real world science hiring, I have not found that equally qualified applicants have faced gender discrimination in terms of wages.  There have been cases where women were not hired for gender issues, but if hiring is done, the wage is consistent with qualifications.  It was not me making the gender-based hiring decisions but I have seen it happen and usually the reason given that women are very sensitive and because of this sensitivity would make existing male employees uncomfortable.  Now regarding the market issue brought up by Mr. Boggs, I do find it quite persuasive except perhaps in very large companies where gender issues are put under a microscope by government agencies and such.  In smaller companies, employers would be crazy to hire a man at a higher rate than a woman of equal quality would demand.  I don't know any market force for ordinary workers that would discourage employers from hiring the cheaper of two equally qualified applicants.
